title: Calculating the test statistic in a t test for a mean
description: Practice calculating the test statistic in a one-sample t test for a mean
topic_path: Carrying out a test for a population mean
Calculating the test statistic in a t test for a mean

assessment_items.question_content:
In Buenos Aires, the average daily high temperature in a year is reported to be $18.2^{\circ}\text{C}$. Catalina believes that last year was warmer than usual. In order to test her belief, she takes a simple random sample of $20$ days from last year and records the daily high temperature on each chosen day. She finds that the sample mean temperature is $19.1^{\circ}\text{C}$ and the sample standard deviation is $7.9^{\circ}\text{C}$.

Catalina wants to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test on the mean. Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Identify the correct test statistic for Catalina's significance test.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample $t$ test for a mean, our test statistics is:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\blueD{\text{statistic}}-\greenD{\text{parameter}}}{\goldD{\text{standard error of statistic}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{\bar x} -\greenD{\mu_0}}{\goldD{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\blueD{\bar x}$ is the sample mean, and the parameter $\greenD{\mu_0}$ is the mean from the null hypothesis. The $\goldD{\text{standard error}}$ of the sample mean is $s_x$ (the sample standard deviation) divided by the square root of $n$ (the sample size).

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=\blueD{19.1}^{\circ}\text{C}$.

Our parameter is the mean from the null hypothesis, so we use $\mu_0=\greenD{18.2}^{\circ}\text{C}$.

The sample standard deviation is $s_x=\maroonD{7.9}^{\circ}\text{C}$.

Since they took a sample of $20$ days, we use $n=\purpleC{20}$.

Those values get plugged in the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\bar x -\mu_0}{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{19.1}-\greenD{18.2}}{\dfrac{\maroonD{7.9}}{\sqrt{\purpleC{20}}}}
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A website streams movies and television shows to its subscribers. Employees know that the average time a user spends per session on their website is $2$ hours. The website changed its design, and they wanted to know if the average session length was longer than $2$ hours. They randomly sampled $50$ users and found that their session lengths had a mean of $2.75$ hours and a standard deviation of $1.55$ hours.

The employees want to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test on the mean. Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Identify the correct test statistic for their significance test.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample $t$ test for a mean, our test statistics is:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\blueD{\text{statistic}}-\greenD{\text{parameter}}}{\goldD{\text{standard error of statistic}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{\bar x} -\greenD{\mu_0}}{\goldD{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\blueD{\bar x}$ is the sample mean, and the parameter $\greenD{\mu_0}$ is the mean from the null hypothesis. The $\goldD{\text{standard error}}$ of the sample mean is $s_x$ (the sample standard deviation) divided by the square root of $n$ (the sample size).

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=\blueD{2.75}$ hours.

Our parameter is the mean from the null hypothesis, so we use $\mu_0=\greenD{2}$ hours.

The sample standard deviation is $s_x=\maroonD{1.55}$ hours.

Since they took a sample of $50$ users, we use $n=\purpleC{50}$.

Those values get plugged in the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\bar x -\mu_0}{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{2.75}-\greenD{2}}{\dfrac{\maroonD{1.55}}{\sqrt{\purpleC{50}}}}
\end{align}$

###Answer

$t=\dfrac{2.75-2}{\dfrac{1.55}{\sqrt{50}}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A fast-food company advertises that the pre-cooked weight for its half-pound burgers is, on average, $0.5\text{ lbs}$. Jayda is in charge of a quality control test that involves weighing a sample of burgers to see if if the sample mean weight is significantly different than $0.5 \text{ lbs}$. She takes a random sample of $30$ burgers and finds a mean weight of $0.54\text{ lbs}$ and a sample standard deviation of $0.02\text{ lbs}$.

Jayda wants to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test on the mean. Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Identify the correct test statistic for Jayda's significance test.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample $t$ test for a mean, our test statistics is:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\blueD{\text{statistic}}-\greenD{\text{parameter}}}{\goldD{\text{standard error of statistic}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{\bar x} -\greenD{\mu_0}}{\goldD{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\blueD{\bar x}$ is the sample mean, and the parameter $\greenD{\mu_0}$ is the mean from the null hypothesis. The $\goldD{\text{standard error}}$ of the sample mean is $s_x$ (the sample standard deviation) divided by the square root of $n$ (the sample size).

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=\blueD{0.54}\text{ lbs}$.

Our parameter is the mean from the null hypothesis, so we use $\mu_0=\greenD{0.5}\text{ lbs}$.

The sample standard deviation is $s_x=\maroonD{0.02}\text{ lbs}$.

Since Jayda took a sample of $30$ burgers, we use $n=\purpleC{30}$.

Those values get plugged in the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\bar x -\mu_0}{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{0.54}-\greenD{0.5}}{\dfrac{\maroonD{0.02}}{\sqrt{\purpleC{30}}}}
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A local pizza store knows the mean amount of time it takes them to deliver an order is $45$ minutes after the order is placed. The manager has a new system for processing delivery orders, and they want to test if it changes the mean delivery time. They take a random sample of $15$ delivery orders and find their mean delivery time is $48$ minutes with a sample standard deviation of $10$ minutes.

The manager wants to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test on the mean. Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Identify the correct test statistic for their significance test.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample $t$ test for a mean, our test statistics is:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\blueD{\text{statistic}}-\greenD{\text{parameter}}}{\goldD{\text{standard error of statistic}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{\bar x} -\greenD{\mu_0}}{\goldD{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\blueD{\bar x}$ is the sample mean, and the parameter $\greenD{\mu_0}$ is the mean from the null hypothesis. The $\goldD{\text{standard error}}$ of the sample mean is $s_x$ (the sample standard deviation) divided by the square root of $n$ (the sample size).

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=\blueD{48}$ minutes.

Our parameter is the mean from the null hypothesis, so we use $\mu_0=\greenD{45}$ minutes.

The sample standard deviation is $s_x=\maroonD{10}$ minutes.

Since they took a sample of $15$ orders, we use $n=\purpleC{15}$.

Those values get plugged in the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\bar x -\mu_0}{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{48}-\greenD{45}}{\dfrac{\maroonD{10}}{\sqrt{\purpleC{15}}}}
\end{align}$

###Answer

$t=\dfrac{48-45}{\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{15}}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A restaurant advertises that its burritos weigh $250\text{ g}$. A consumer advocacy group doubts this claim, and they obtain a random sample of $24$ of these burritos to test if the mean weight is significantly lower than $250\text{ g}$. They calculate a sample mean weight of $242\text{ g}$ and a sample standard deviation of $12\text{ g}$.

The advocacy group wants to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test on the mean. Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Identify the correct test statistic for their significance test.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample $t$ test for a mean, our test statistics is:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\blueD{\text{statistic}}-\greenD{\text{parameter}}}{\goldD{\text{standard error of statistic}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{\bar x} -\greenD{\mu_0}}{\goldD{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\blueD{\bar x}$ is the sample mean, and the parameter $\greenD{\mu_0}$ is the mean from the null hypothesis. The $\goldD{\text{standard error}}$ of the sample mean is $s_x$ (the sample standard deviation) divided by the square root of $n$ (the sample size).

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=\blueD{242}\text{ g}$.

Our parameter is the mean from the null hypothesis, so we use $\mu_0=\greenD{250}\text{ g}$.

The sample standard deviation is $s_x=\maroonD{12}\text{ g}$.

Since they took a sample of $24$ burritos, we use $n=\purpleC{24}$.

Those values get plugged in the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\bar x -\mu_0}{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{242}-\greenD{250}}{\dfrac{\maroonD{12}}{\sqrt{\purpleC{24}}}}
\end{align}$

###Answer

$t=\dfrac{242-250}{\dfrac{12}{\sqrt{24}}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A company advertises that its cans of caviar each contain $100\text{ g}$ of their product. A consumer advocacy group doubts this claim, and they obtain a random sample of $8$ cans to test if the mean weight is significantly lower than $100\text{ g}$. They calculate a sample mean weight of $99\text{ g}$ and a sample standard deviation of $0.9\text{ g}$.

The advocacy group wants to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test on the mean. Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Identify the correct test statistic for their significance test.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample $t$ test for a mean, our test statistics is:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\blueD{\text{statistic}}-\greenD{\text{parameter}}}{\goldD{\text{standard error of statistic}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{\bar x} -\greenD{\mu_0}}{\goldD{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\blueD{\bar x}$ is the sample mean, and the parameter $\greenD{\mu_0}$ is the mean from the null hypothesis. The $\goldD{\text{standard error}}$ of the sample mean is $s_x$ (the sample standard deviation) divided by the square root of $n$ (the sample size).

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=\blueD{99}\text{ g}$.

Our parameter is the mean from the null hypothesis, so we use $\mu_0=\greenD{100}\text{ g}$.

The sample standard deviation is $s_x=\maroonD{0.9}\text{ g}$.

Since they took a sample of $8$ cans, we use $n=\purpleC{8}$.

Those values get plugged in the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\bar x -\mu_0}{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{99}-\greenD{100}}{\dfrac{\maroonD{0.9}}{\sqrt{\purpleC{8}}}}
\end{align}$

###Answer

$t=\dfrac{99-100}{\dfrac{0.9}{\sqrt{8}}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
St. West High School requires that students are assigned an average of $2.5$ hours of homework each night. The school board believes that the average amount of time per night is not $2.5$ hours, so they decide to take a random sample of $16$ students to see if there is convincing evidence to support their suspicion. The board finds that the sample of students were assigned an average of $2.8$ hours of homework per night with a standard deviation of $0.8$ hours.

The board want to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test on the mean. Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Calculate the test statistic for their test.**  
*You may round your answer to two decimal places.*

$t=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample $t$ test for a mean, our test statistics is:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\blueD{\text{statistic}}-\greenD{\text{parameter}}}{\goldD{\text{standard error of statistic}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{\bar x} -\greenD{\mu_0}}{\goldD{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\blueD{\bar x}$ is the sample mean, and the parameter $\greenD{\mu_0}$ is the mean from the null hypothesis. The $\goldD{\text{standard error}}$ of the sample mean is $s_x$ (the sample standard deviation) divided by the square root of $n$ (the sample size).

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=\blueD{2.8}$ hours.

Our parameter is the mean from the null hypothesis, so we use $\mu_0=\greenD{2.5}$ hours.

The sample standard deviation is $s_x=\maroonD{0.8}$ hours.

Since they took a sample of $16$ students, we use $n=\purpleC{16}$.

Those values get plugged in the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\bar x -\mu_0}{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{2.8}-\greenD{2.5}}{\dfrac{\maroonD{0.8}}{\sqrt{\purpleC{16}}}}
\end{align}$

###Calculations

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{2.8-2.5}{\dfrac{0.8}{\sqrt{16}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{0.3}{0.2} \\\\
&= 1.5
\end{align}$

###Answer

$t=1.5$

assessment_items.question_content:
Fernanda runs a large bowling league. She suspects that the league average score is greater than $150$ per game. She takes a random sample of $36$ game scores from the league data. The scores in the sample have a mean of $156$ and a standard deviation of $30$.

Fernanda wants to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test on the mean. Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Calculate the test statistic for Fernanda's test.**  
*You may round your answer to two decimal places.*

$t=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample $t$ test for a mean, our test statistics is:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\blueD{\text{statistic}}-\greenD{\text{parameter}}}{\goldD{\text{standard error of statistic}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{\bar x} -\greenD{\mu_0}}{\goldD{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\blueD{\bar x}$ is the sample mean, and the parameter $\greenD{\mu_0}$ is the mean from the null hypothesis. The $\goldD{\text{standard error}}$ of the sample mean is $s_x$ (the sample standard deviation) divided by the square root of $n$ (the sample size).

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=\blueD{156}$.

Our parameter is the mean from the null hypothesis, so we use $\mu_0=\greenD{150}$.

The sample standard deviation is $s_x=\maroonD{30}$.

Since Fernanda took a sample of $36$ scores, we use $n=\purpleC{36}$.

Those values get plugged in the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\bar x -\mu_0}{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{156}-\greenD{150}}{\dfrac{\maroonD{30}}{\sqrt{\purpleC{36}}}}
\end{align}$

###Calculations

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{156-150}{\dfrac{30}{\sqrt{36}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{6}{5} \\\\
&= 1.2
\end{align}$

###Answer

$t=1.2$

assessment_items.question_content:
Adam is a golfer who thinks that his drives have an average length of more than $200\text{ m}$. He decided to test $\text{H}_0: \mu=200\text{ m}$ versus $\text{H}_\text{a}:\mu>200\text{ m}$ using a sample of $49$ drives. His sample mean distance was $208\text{ m}$ and his sample standard deviation was $25\text{ m}$.

Adam wants to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test on the mean. Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Calculate the test statistic for Adam's test.**  
*You may round your answer to two decimal places.*

$t=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample $t$ test for a mean, our test statistics is:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\blueD{\text{statistic}}-\greenD{\text{parameter}}}{\goldD{\text{standard error of statistic}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{\bar x} -\greenD{\mu_0}}{\goldD{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\blueD{\bar x}$ is the sample mean, and the parameter $\greenD{\mu_0}$ is the mean from the null hypothesis. The $\goldD{\text{standard error}}$ of the sample mean is $s_x$ (the sample standard deviation) divided by the square root of $n$ (the sample size).

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=\blueD{208}\text{ m}$.

Our parameter is the mean from the null hypothesis, so we use $\mu_0=\greenD{200}\text{ m}$.

The sample standard deviation is $s_x=\maroonD{25}\text{ m}$.

Since Adam took a sample of $49$ drives, we use $n=\purpleC{49}$.

Those values get plugged in the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\bar x -\mu_0}{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{208}-\greenD{200}}{\dfrac{\maroonD{25}}{\sqrt{\purpleC{49}}}}
\end{align}$

###Calculations

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{208-200}{\dfrac{25}{\sqrt{49}}} \\\\
&= \dfrac{8}{\left(\dfrac{25}{7}\right)} \\\\
&=8 \cdot \dfrac{7}{25} \\\\
&= 2.24
\end{align}$

###Answer

$t=2.24$

assessment_items.question_content:
Rory suspects that teachers in his school district have less than $5$ years of experience on average. He decided to test $\text{H}_0: \mu=5$ versus $\text{H}_\text{a}:\mu<5$ using a sample of $25$ teachers. His sample mean was $4$ years and his sample standard deviation was $2$ years.

Rory wants to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test on the mean. Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Calculate the test statistic for Rory's test.**  
*You may round your answer to two decimal places.*

$t=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample $t$ test for a mean, our test statistics is:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\blueD{\text{statistic}}-\greenD{\text{parameter}}}{\goldD{\text{standard error of statistic}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{\bar x} -\greenD{\mu_0}}{\goldD{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\blueD{\bar x}$ is the sample mean, and the parameter $\greenD{\mu_0}$ is the mean from the null hypothesis. The $\goldD{\text{standard error}}$ of the sample mean is $s_x$ (the sample standard deviation) divided by the square root of $n$ (the sample size).

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=\blueD{4}$ years.

Our parameter is the mean from the null hypothesis, so we use $\mu_0=\greenD{5}$ years.

The sample standard deviation is $s_x=\maroonD{2}$ years.

Since Rory took a sample of $25$ teachers, we use $n=\purpleC{25}$.

Those values get plugged in the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\bar x -\mu_0}{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{4}-\greenD{5}}{\dfrac{\maroonD{2}}{\sqrt{\purpleC{25}}}}
\end{align}$

###Calculations

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{4-5}{\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{25}}} \\\\
&= \dfrac{-1}{\left(\dfrac{2}{5}\right)} \\\\
&=-1 \cdot \dfrac{5}{2} \\\\
&= -2.5
\end{align}$

###Answer

$t=-2.5$

assessment_items.question_content:
Jumbo eggs in Australia, on average, are supposed to weigh $68\text{ g}$. Margot is in charge of a quality control test that involves weighing a sample of eggs to see if the sample mean weight is significantly different than $68 \text{ g}$. She takes a random sample of $100$ eggs and finds a mean weight of $67\text{ g}$ and a sample standard deviation of $1\text{ g}$.

Margot wants to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test on the mean. Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Calculate the test statistic for Margot's test.**  
*You may round your answer to two decimal places.*

$t=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample $t$ test for a mean, our test statistics is:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\blueD{\text{statistic}}-\greenD{\text{parameter}}}{\goldD{\text{standard error of statistic}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{\bar x} -\greenD{\mu_0}}{\goldD{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\blueD{\bar x}$ is the sample mean, and the parameter $\greenD{\mu_0}$ is the mean from the null hypothesis. The $\goldD{\text{standard error}}$ of the sample mean is $s_x$ (the sample standard deviation) divided by the square root of $n$ (the sample size).

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=\blueD{67}\text{ g}$.

Our parameter is the mean from the null hypothesis, so we use $\mu_0=\greenD{68}\text{ g}$.

The sample standard deviation is $s_x=\maroonD{1}\text{ g}$.

Since Margot took a sample of $100$ eggs, we use $n=\purpleC{100}$.

Those values get plugged in the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\bar x -\mu_0}{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{67}-\greenD{68}}{\dfrac{\maroonD{1}}{\sqrt{\purpleC{100}}}}
\end{align}$

###Calculations

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{67-68}{\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{100}}} \\\\
&= \dfrac{-1}{\left(\dfrac{1}{10}\right)} \\\\
&=-1 \cdot \dfrac{10}{1} \\\\
&= -10
\end{align}$

###Answer

$t=-10$

assessment_items.question_content:
A factory fills bottles with a beverage, and each bottle is supposed to contain $500\text{ mL}$. Norah is in charge of a quality control test that involves measuring the amounts in a sample of bottles to see if the sample mean amount is significantly different than $500 \text{ mL}$. She takes a random sample of $16$ bottles and finds a mean amount of $497\text{ mL}$ and a sample standard deviation of $6\text{ mL}$.

Norah wants to use these sample data to conduct a $t$ test on the mean. Assume that all conditions for inference have been met.

**Calculate the test statistic for Norah's test.**  
*You may round your answer to two decimal places.*

$t=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###The formula for the test statistic

The test statistic gives us an idea of how far away our sample result is from our null hypothesis. For a one-sample $t$ test for a mean, our test statistics is:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\blueD{\text{statistic}}-\greenD{\text{parameter}}}{\goldD{\text{standard error of statistic}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{\bar x} -\greenD{\mu_0}}{\goldD{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}}}
\end{align}$

The statistic $\blueD{\bar x}$ is the sample mean, and the parameter $\greenD{\mu_0}$ is the mean from the null hypothesis. The $\goldD{\text{standard error}}$ of the sample mean is $s_x$ (the sample standard deviation) divided by the square root of $n$ (the sample size).

###Components of formula

Our statistic is the sample mean $\bar x=\blueD{497}\text{ mL}$.

Our parameter is the mean from the null hypothesis, so we use $\mu_0=\greenD{500}\text{ mL}$.

The sample standard deviation is $s_x=\maroonD{6}\text{ mL}$.

Since Norah took a sample of $16$ bottles, we use $n=\purpleC{16}$.

Those values get plugged in the test statistic formula:

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{\bar x -\mu_0}{\dfrac{s_x}{\sqrt{n}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{497}-\greenD{500}}{\dfrac{\maroonD{6}}{\sqrt{\purpleC{16}}}}
\end{align}$

###Calculations

$\begin{align}
t&=\dfrac{497-500}{\dfrac{6}{\sqrt{16}}} \\\\
&= \dfrac{-3}{1.5} \\\\
&= -2
\end{align}$

###Answer

$t=-2$
